column2: # For each polygon in DeSo, this extracts raster values inside the polygon, ignores NA cells, calculates the 95th percentile of temperature values and stores the results. In other words it gives a measure of heat intensity within each polygon. The percentile is based only on extracted cell values, not weighted by how much of each cell lies inside the polygon.
# Mean LST of "hot" cells only >= threshold

# For each polygon, this command keeps only cells where temperature is at least 30 °C, returns NA if there are no such cells and calculates the coverage-weighted mean of those hot-cell temperatures. The results tell you, within the hot parts of a polygon only, what the average hot temperature is.
# Exposure extent, fraction of covered area that is hot >= threshold

# For each polygon, we find cells with non-missing values and temperature at least 30 °, sum their coverage fractions and multiplie by cell area. This gives us the hot area in square meters inside the polygon.
sum(coverage_fraction[!is.na(values) & values >= hot_thr], na.rm = TRUE) * cell_area

# Finally we sum the coverage fractions of all non-NA raster cells inside the polygon and multiply them by cell area. This is the area of the polygon that is actually covered by valid raster data. That is important because some parts of a polygon may fall outside the raster or on NA cells.
# Fraction and percent of covered area that is hot
# This chunk adds two new columns:
# hot_frac_cov: proportion of raster-covered area that is hot
# hot_percent_cov: the same value in percent, rounded to 2 decimals
# It gives the extent of heat exposure within the valid raster-covered part of each polygon.
# And then we print the results
Simple feature collection with 159 features and 35 fields
